title: AI-Driven Predictive Maintenance Platform for Mid-Sized Manufacturing Plants
summary: A mid-sized automotive parts manufacturer is seeking a commercially ready, AI-based predictive maintenance platform that can integrate with existing PLCs and SCADA systems, aggregate sensor data, and provide early-failure warnings for CNC machines, presses, and conveyor systems. The goal is to reduce unplanned downtime by at least 30% and optimize maintenance scheduling across two plants in Ohio and Michigan.
comments_regarding_stage_of_development:
We are not looking for a solution at the concept or prototype stage. We specifically require a mature, commercially available platform that has already been field-tested in comparable industrial environments (discrete manufacturing, not just process industries). Vendors should be able to provide at least 2–3 reference deployments in plants with 100+ machines.

We have already initiated an internal project to consolidate machine data from our existing Siemens and Allen-Bradley PLCs and to standardize asset IDs across our Toledo (OH) and Lansing (MI) facilities. A preliminary requirements document and budgetary approval have been secured, and we are now in active discussions with several potential providers. Because our current unplanned downtime is directly impacting a new supply contract with a major OEM, the negotiations and selection process are time-sensitive and considered urgent internally.

We are open to a phased rollout, starting with a pilot line in the Toledo plant, followed by a broader deployment across both sites within 12–18 months, provided the initial KPIs are met.
description:
Persona / Organization Context:
The requesting organization is Great Lakes Precision Components, Inc., a privately held Tier-2 supplier of machined and stamped components for the automotive industry. The company operates two plants: the primary facility in Toledo, Ohio (approx. 280 employees) and a secondary facility in Lansing, Michigan (approx. 140 employees). The request is being submitted by Daniel M. Herrera, Director of Operations, who has been with the company for 9 years and oversees production, maintenance, and continuous improvement initiatives across both sites.

Current Situation and Pain Points:
Over the last 24 months, Great Lakes Precision Components has experienced a noticeable increase in unplanned equipment downtime, particularly on:
- CNC machining centers (Mazak and Haas, 3–7 years old)
- Mechanical and hydraulic stamping presses (ranging from 250–600 tons)
- Conveyor systems and automated part-handling equipment connected to robotic cells

Root-cause analyses have shown that many of these breakdowns could have been anticipated with better condition monitoring and trend analysis (e.g., spindle bearing failures, hydraulic leaks, overheating of servo drives, gradual misalignment, and lubrication issues). Currently, maintenance is largely preventive and calendar-based, with some reactive firefighting. The in-house maintenance team uses a basic CMMS mainly for work order tracking, but there is no systematic use of sensor data to predict failures.

Unplanned downtime in 2024 has averaged 9.5% of available production hours on critical lines, compared to a target of 5%. This has led to:
- Missed delivery windows for two key customers
- Increased overtime costs (both maintenance technicians and operators)
- Higher scrap rates during restart after breakdowns
- Strain on the relationship with a new OEM customer that has strict on-time delivery penalties

Business Objective of the Technology Request:
The primary objective is to identify and implement an AI-driven predictive maintenance platform that can:
1. Reduce unplanned downtime on critical assets by at least 30% within 18 months of deployment.
2. Enable data-driven maintenance scheduling (moving from time-based to condition-based and predictive strategies).
3. Provide clear, actionable alerts with enough lead time (ideally 7–14 days) to plan interventions without disrupting customer deliveries.
4. Integrate with existing automation and IT infrastructure without requiring a complete overhaul of current systems.

Technical and Integration Requirements:
1. Data Sources and Connectivity:
   - The platform must be able to ingest data from existing PLCs (primarily Siemens S7 and Allen-Bradley ControlLogix) and, where needed, directly from sensors (vibration, temperature, current, pressure, etc.).
   - Support for common industrial protocols such as OPC UA, Modbus TCP, and Ethernet/IP is mandatory.
   - The current network architecture is segmented, with a dedicated OT network in each plant. Any proposed solution must respect this segmentation and align with our existing cybersecurity policies.

2. Types of Equipment to Monitor (Initial Scope):
   - 18 CNC machining centers (spindles, axes, coolant systems, drives)
   - 12 stamping presses (clutch/brake systems, tonnage, slide motion, lubrication systems)
   - 6 conveyor/transfer lines (motors, gearboxes, belts, sensors)
   - Ancillary equipment such as air compressors and central hydraulic power units

3. Analytics and AI Capabilities:
   - The solution should provide machine-learning-based anomaly detection and failure prediction models that can either use vendor-provided pre-trained models or be trained on our historical and ongoing data.
   - It must support trending, correlation analysis, and root-cause analysis tools accessible to both engineers and experienced maintenance technicians (not just data scientists).
   - The system should allow configuration of custom rules (e.g., thresholds, combinations of sensor behaviors) in addition to AI-driven insights, so that existing tribal knowledge from senior technicians can be codified.

4. User Interface and Usability:
   - A web-based dashboard accessible from both plants, with role-based access control (maintenance, production supervisors, plant managers).
   - Clear visualization of asset health, remaining useful life estimates (where available), and prioritized alert lists.
   - Mobile access (tablet or smartphone) is highly desirable, as technicians frequently move between lines.

5. Deployment Model and IT Constraints:
   - We are open to either an on-premises or hybrid cloud deployment. However, due to customer audit requirements and existing IT policies, purely cloud-hosted solutions that require constant outbound data streams may face approval challenges.
   - Data residency and security are important. The vendor must comply with standard cybersecurity frameworks (e.g., ISO 27001 or equivalent) and support secure communication (TLS, VPN, etc.).

6. Integration with Existing Systems:
   - The platform should integrate with or provide APIs for our existing CMMS (Fiix) and, ideally, our ERP system (Epicor) to trigger or suggest work orders based on predicted failures.
   - Basic export capabilities (CSV, Excel, or database connectors) are necessary for periodic reporting and internal audits.

Stage of Development Expectations for the Requested Technology:
We are specifically interested in vendors whose predictive maintenance platform is already in commercial use in similar industrial environments. The solution should:
- Be fully productized, with documentation, training materials, and a defined support structure.
- Have at least 2–3 years of real-world operation in manufacturing plants (preferably automotive or metalworking) with documented case studies or references.
- Be available for demonstration—either via remote demo using anonymized real plant data or, ideally, an on-site or pilot deployment in our Toledo facility.

We are not interested in early-stage research projects, prototypes that are still under development, or solutions that require us to act as the primary test site for unproven algorithms. Our internal project has already moved beyond the conceptual phase; we have:
- Mapped our critical assets and tagged them in our CMMS.
- Conducted preliminary data audits to understand what signals are currently available from PLCs and what additional sensors may be needed.
- Allocated a preliminary budget and received executive approval to proceed to vendor selection.

Timeline and Urgency:
The urgency of this request is driven by a recently signed, three-year supply agreement with a major automotive OEM. The contract includes strict delivery and quality metrics, with financial penalties for late shipments beyond a narrow grace period. Two of the lines that will produce parts for this new contract have been among the worst performers in terms of unplanned downtime.

Our target timeline is as follows:
- Vendor shortlisting and initial demos: within the next 2–3 months.
- Pilot implementation on one machining line and one press line in Toledo: within 6–9 months.
- Evaluation of pilot KPIs (downtime reduction, forecast accuracy, user adoption): by month 12.
- Gradual rollout to remaining critical assets in Toledo and Lansing: months 12–24.

We would prefer to begin negotiations and technical workshops with shortlisted vendors as soon as possible, ideally within the current fiscal quarter, to align with budget cycles and internal resource planning.

Additional Preferences and Constraints:
- Training and Change Management: The selected vendor should be prepared to support on-site or virtual training sessions for maintenance technicians, controls engineers, and production supervisors. Our workforce includes a mix of experienced technicians (20+ years) and newer hires, so the training should address different comfort levels with digital tools.
- Licensing and Cost Structure: We are open to subscription-based licensing (per asset or per site) but need cost transparency for scaling from an initial pilot to full deployment. Hidden costs for connectors, additional modules, or data storage are a concern.
- Support and Service: We require at least business-hours support in Eastern Time (with clear SLAs) and a defined escalation path. Experience working with unionized maintenance workforces is a plus, as both plants are partially unionized.

In summary, Great Lakes Precision Components is actively seeking a mature, field-proven predictive maintenance technology that can be demonstrated in the near term and deployed in phases across two manufacturing plants. The technology must integrate with our existing automation and IT landscape, provide actionable and reliable predictions, and help us materially reduce unplanned downtime and associated costs, while supporting our obligations under a new, strategically important OEM contract.
